enrichment.short_description: Enterprise business intelligence platform from Google Cloud built on the LookML semantic modeling layer for governed analytics.
enrichment.company_size: Part of Google Cloud (Alphabet); Looker had roughly 800 employees at the time of acquisition
enrichment.headquarters: Mountain View, California, US (Google Cloud); originally founded in Santa Cruz, California
enrichment.seo_title: Looker Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Looker review 2026: how Google Cloud's LookML-based BI platform works, embedded analytics, pricing structure, and how it compares to Tableau and Power BI.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Looker is an enterprise business intelligence and data platform now owned by Google and sold through Google Cloud. It was founded in January 2012 in Santa Cruz, California, by Lloyd Tabb and Ben Porterfield, who had previously worked on data and infrastructure projects at companies including Netscape and LiveOps. Looker's founding idea was that traditional BI tools forced analysts into static, disconnected reports built from copied-out data extracts. Instead, Looker introduced LookML, a version-controlled, git-backed modeling language that lets data teams define metrics, dimensions, and table relationships once in a central semantic layer. Business users then explore that governed data directly against the live warehouse rather than a stale cube, running native SQL against systems like BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift, and PostgreSQL. This in-database, semantic-layer-first architecture set Looker apart from legacy BI incumbents and helped it grow quickly among data-driven technology and SaaS companies during the mid-2010s. Google acquired Looker in 2019 for approximately 2.6 billion dollars, with the deal closing in February 2020. Since then, Looker has become the umbrella brand for Google Cloud's business intelligence portfolio, and the underlying LookML semantic layer has increasingly been connected to Gemini-powered conversational analytics. Looker's free, self-serve sibling product was rebranded from Google Data Studio to Looker Studio in October 2022, then reverted to the Data Studio name in April 2026, a change that does not affect the enterprise Looker platform itself. Architecturally, Looker targets centralized data teams rather than individual report builders. Analysts and data engineers write LookML models describing dimensions, measures, joins, and row- or column-level access rules; business users then browse, filter, and visualize that governed data through Looker's Explore interface without writing SQL. The platform supports scheduled delivery and alerting, a full REST API and SDKs for building custom data applications on top of the semantic layer, and an embedded analytics offering that lets SaaS companies white-label Looker dashboards inside their own products. Core features include the LookML modeling language as a single reusable semantic layer; native, extract-free querying against modern cloud warehouses; embedded and white-labeled analytics with granular access controls; an extensive API and developer platform; enterprise-grade governance and security, including row-level permissions; scheduled reports and Slack/email integrations; and generative-AI features such as natural-language querying layered on top of the governed model. Pricing is not published; Looker is sold through custom, quote-based enterprise contracts negotiated with the Google Cloud sales team, typically structured around platform access and the number of viewer and developer seats, with BigQuery or other warehouse compute billed separately. This makes Looker a considered enterprise purchase aimed at mid-market and large organizations, dedicated data platform teams, and software companies embedding analytics into their own products, rather than individual marketers or small teams, who are instead better served by Looker's own free sibling product or lighter self-serve dashboard tools. Looker's main competitors are other enterprise BI and semantic-layer platforms, including Tableau (owned by Salesforce), Microsoft Power BI, Qlik Sense, Domo, Sisense, and newer standalone semantic-layer challengers such as Cube and Omni. Looker differentiates itself through the maturity of LookML as a portable, version-controlled modeling layer; deep native integration with BigQuery and the broader Google Cloud AI stack; and strong embedded-analytics tooling for software vendors. Its main trade-offs are a steeper implementation curve, since LookML modeling requires dedicated data engineering investment before business users see value, and enterprise-only custom pricing that puts it out of reach for smaller teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Looker is a governed business intelligence platform built around LookML, a version-controlled semantic modeling language, and sold as part of Google Cloud since Google's 2.6 billion dollar acquisition of the company in 2019.
Rather than working from static extracts, Looker queries live data directly in the warehouse, which keeps dashboards and embedded analytics in sync with the source system at all times.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: LookML lets data teams define metrics, joins, and access rules once, then reuse that governed logic across every dashboard, embedded app, and ad hoc exploration in the organization.

Looker's embed SDK and API are widely used by SaaS companies that want to white-label analytics inside their own customer-facing products, turning Looker into an application-development platform as much as a reporting tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Looker does not publish self-serve pricing; access is sold through custom Google Cloud enterprise contracts based on platform edition, number of seats, and connected data volume.
Because pricing requires a sales conversation, Looker is generally a fit for organizations that already have a dedicated data or analytics engineering function to build out the LookML model.

features.title: Embedded analytics
features.description: White-labeled dashboards and visualizations can be embedded into third-party products via Looker's embed SDK and API.

features.title: Governed access controls
features.description: Row-level and column-level permissions let enterprises enforce data security and compliance at the semantic layer.

features.title: Looker Platform and API
features.description: A full REST API and extension framework for building custom data applications on top of governed Looker models.

features.title: Scheduled delivery and alerting
features.description: Automated report delivery, alerts, and integrations with Slack and email keep stakeholders updated without manual exports.

features.title: Google Cloud and Gemini integration
features.description: Deep native connectivity to BigQuery and increasing integration with Gemini-powered conversational analytics.

features.title: Multi-cloud connectivity
features.description: Connects beyond Google Cloud to Snowflake, Redshift, PostgreSQL, and other major warehouses.

pros: Version-controlled LookML semantic layer keeps metrics consistent across the whole organization
Strong embedded-analytics tooling for SaaS companies white-labeling dashboards
Queries live warehouse data instead of stale extracts
Deep native integration with BigQuery and Google Cloud's AI stack
Enterprise-grade governance with row-level and column-level security
cons: Pricing is entirely custom and requires a sales conversation, with no public self-serve tier
Steep implementation curve; LookML modeling requires dedicated data engineering resources
Overkill and cost-prohibitive for small teams or individual analysts
Learning LookML syntax adds onboarding time compared to drag-and-drop BI tools
Full value depends on a mature underlying data warehouse being in place first

faqs.question: Is Looker the same product as Looker Studio
faqs.answer: No. Looker is Google Cloud's paid, enterprise semantic-layer BI platform built on LookML, while Looker Studio (renamed Data Studio again in April 2026) is a separate, free self-serve reporting and visualization tool. They share a brand history but are different products for different audiences.

faqs.question: Who founded Looker
faqs.answer: Looker was founded in January 2012 in Santa Cruz, California, by Lloyd Tabb and Ben Porterfield.

faqs.question: When did Google acquire Looker
faqs.answer: Google announced its acquisition of Looker in June 2019 for approximately 2.6 billion dollars, with the deal closing in February 2020.

faqs.question: What is LookML
faqs.answer: LookML is Looker's proprietary, version-controlled modeling language used to define dimensions, measures, and relationships in a governed semantic layer that sits between raw warehouse tables and business users.

faqs.question: How much does Looker cost
faqs.answer: Looker does not publish pricing publicly. It is sold through custom enterprise contracts negotiated with the Google Cloud sales team, based on edition, seats, and data volume.

faqs.question: Who is Looker built for
faqs.answer: Looker targets mid-market and large enterprises with dedicated data teams, plus software companies that want to embed governed analytics inside their own products.

use_cases.title: Embedded analytics for SaaS products

use_cases.description: Large organizations use LookML to define a single source of truth for metrics that every department queries consistently.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise data and analytics engineering teams

use_cases.title: Governed self-service exploration
use_cases.description: Business users explore live warehouse data through Looker's Explore interface without writing SQL, while access stays governed by the semantic layer.

features: Dedicated Google Cloud sales and support

enrichment.short_description: Google's free data visualization and reporting tool, formerly Google Data Studio and later renamed back to Data Studio in 2026.
enrichment.seo_title: Looker Studio Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Looker Studio review 2026: free reporting and dashboard features, the Pro tier, its rename history from Data Studio, and how it differs from Looker.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Looker Studio is Google's free, self-serve data visualization and reporting tool, and it is a completely different product from Looker despite the shared branding history and similar name. The tool launched on March 15, 2016 as Google Data Studio, initially bundled into the paid Google Analytics 360 enterprise suite, before Google released a free version for individuals and small teams in May 2016. Data Studio quickly became a popular way for marketers, analysts, and agencies to build interactive dashboards from Google Analytics, Google Ads, Google Sheets, BigQuery, and hundreds of third-party connectors, without writing code. In June 2019 Google acquired the enterprise BI company Looker for roughly 2.6 billion dollars, with the deal closing in February 2020; at Google Cloud Next in October 2022, Google unified its business intelligence branding and renamed Data Studio to Looker Studio, simultaneously introducing a new paid tier, Looker Studio Pro, aimed at teams needing enterprise management, security, and collaboration features on top of the free product. That branding lasted roughly three and a half years: on April 11, 2026, Google reversed course and renamed Looker Studio back to Data Studio, repositioning it as a unified hub for personal and team-level data assets across Google's data ecosystem, including Data Studio reports, BigQuery conversational AI agents, and data apps built in Colab notebooks. Existing reports, data sources, and users were transitioned automatically with no migration required. Throughout all of these name changes, the underlying free reporting product and the separate paid Looker enterprise BI platform have remained functionally distinct: Looker (BI) is a governed, LookML-based semantic-layer platform sold through custom enterprise contracts, while Looker Studio, now Data Studio again, is the free, individual-oriented reporting and dashboard tool that any Google account holder can use immediately. Architecturally, the product is a browser-based report builder that connects to live or cached data sources through native and third-party connectors, lets users drag in charts, tables, and controls, and supports sharing and embedding much like Google Docs or Sheets. Data blending allows multiple sources to be combined into one chart, and community connectors extend it to platforms like Meta Ads, LinkedIn, HubSpot, and Semrush, typically through third-party connector services such as Supermetrics or Funnel that carry their own separate fees. Core capabilities include a free tier with unlimited reports for individual or small-team use, connections to essentially all Google marketing and data products, real-time collaborative editing, embeddable reports for websites, and a growing set of AI-assisted features for generating and explaining visualizations. The paid Pro tier, priced at roughly 9 dollars per user per project per month on annual billing, adds team workspace management, content approval workflows, enterprise-grade security and admin controls, and deeper integration with Google Cloud and Workspace admin consoles; a five-user, one-project Pro deployment runs around 540 dollars a year, and pricing scales per licensed (not just active) user. The product's target audience is broad: individual marketers and analysts building ad hoc reports, in-house teams standardizing recurring dashboards, and agencies delivering client reporting, all of whom want visualization power without Looker's enterprise implementation overhead. Competitors in the free-to-low-cost reporting space include Tableau Public, Microsoft Power BI (particularly its free desktop tier), Domo, and newer AI-native dashboard tools; the tool's core differentiation is that it is free for individual use, deeply native to the rest of the Google product ecosystem (Analytics, Ads, Sheets, BigQuery), and requires no infrastructure or IT provisioning to get started, trading some of the governance and modeling rigor that the separate, paid Looker platform offers to larger enterprises.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Looker Studio began life in 2016 as Google Data Studio, was renamed Looker Studio in October 2022 as part of Google's BI brand unification, and reverted to the Data Studio name again in April 2026.
It is a free, self-serve reporting and dashboard tool, distinct from the paid enterprise Looker BI platform, even though both now sit under Google's data analytics umbrella.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The tool connects natively to Google Analytics, Google Ads, Sheets, and BigQuery, and to hundreds of third-party sources through community and partner connectors.
Data blending lets users combine multiple sources into a single chart, while sharing and embedding work much like Google Docs, making it easy to distribute reports without extra infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core reporting product remains free for unlimited individual use, connecting to Google and third-party data sources at no cost.
A paid Pro tier, priced per user per project and billed around 9 dollars per user monthly on an annual plan, adds team workspace management, approval workflows, and enterprise security controls.

features.title: Looker Studio Pro (paid tier)
features.description: Adds team content management, approval workflows, and enterprise security for organizations scaling report usage.

features.title: Third-party connector marketplace
features.description: Community and partner connectors extend reach to platforms like Meta Ads, HubSpot, and Semrush.

features.title: AI-assisted report building
features.description: Emerging generative-AI tools help generate charts, explain data, and speed up report creation.

pros: Core reporting product is completely free with no usage cap for individuals
Deep native integration with Google Analytics, Ads, Sheets, and BigQuery
No infrastructure or IT setup required to start building reports
Easy sharing and embedding modeled on familiar Google Docs-style permissions
Large community connector ecosystem for non-Google data sources
cons: Non-Google data sources often require paid third-party connectors like Supermetrics or Funnel
Lacks the governed semantic layer and row-level security of the paid Looker BI platform
Performance can lag on large or complex data blends
Brand has changed names twice in under four years, causing confusion for users and documentation
Pro tier billing is per licensed user per project, which can get expensive for multi-project agencies

faqs.question: Is Looker Studio the same as Looker
faqs.answer: No. Looker Studio (now renamed back to Data Studio) is a free, individual-oriented reporting tool, while Looker is a separate, paid enterprise BI platform built on LookML and sold through custom Google Cloud contracts.

faqs.question: Why was Looker Studio renamed back to Data Studio
faqs.answer: On April 11, 2026, Google reversed its 2022 rebrand and renamed Looker Studio back to Data Studio, positioning it as a unified hub for reports, BigQuery AI agents, and Colab data apps across Google's data ecosystem.

faqs.question: Is Looker Studio actually free
faqs.answer: Yes, the core reporting and visualization product is free for unlimited individual use. A paid Pro tier exists for teams that need workspace management, approval workflows, and enterprise security.

faqs.question: What was Looker Studio previously called
faqs.answer: It launched in 2016 as Google Data Studio, was renamed Looker Studio in October 2022, and was renamed back to Data Studio in April 2026.

faqs.question: Does Looker Studio connect to non-Google data sources
faqs.answer: Yes, through community and partner connectors, though many non-Google integrations (such as Meta Ads or HubSpot) require a separate paid connector service.

faqs.question: How much does the paid Pro tier cost
faqs.answer: Looker Studio Pro is priced at roughly 9 dollars per user per project per month on annual billing, charged per licensed seat regardless of activity.

enrichment.short_description: Async video messaging and screen recording tool for team communication, acquired by Atlassian in 2023.
enrichment.company_size: Roughly 280 to 350 employees (2026), part of Atlassian
enrichment.seo_title: Loom Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Loom review 2026: async screen recording and video messaging features, pricing tiers, the Atlassian acquisition, and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Loom is an asynchronous video messaging and screen recording tool that lets people record their screen, webcam, or both, then instantly share a link instead of scheduling a live meeting or writing a long message. It was founded in San Francisco in 2015 by Vinay Hiremath, Shahed Khan, and Joe Thomas, initially as a lightweight browser extension for quick screen captures aimed at customer support and sales teams. The product found strong product-market fit during the shift to remote and hybrid work in the late 2010s and especially during the COVID-19 pandemic, when async video became a substitute for many synchronous meetings. Loom grew to roughly 25 million users and more than 5 million video conversations recorded per month, raising around 200 million dollars in venture funding from investors including Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz, ICONIQ, Coatue, and Kleiner Perkins, reaching a valuation of about 1.5 billion dollars in 2022. On October 12, 2023, Atlassian announced it would acquire Loom for 975 million dollars, a deal that closed on November 30, 2023. The acquisition brought Loom into Atlassian's collaboration suite alongside Jira, Confluence, and Trello, positioning async video as a complement to Atlassian's existing text-based and ticketing-based workflows, while Loom has continued to operate as a distinct, standalone product and brand rather than being folded entirely into another Atlassian tool. Architecturally, Loom is a cloud-based recording and hosting platform: a lightweight desktop app, browser extension, or mobile app captures screen and camera video, automatically uploads it, and generates a shareable link with viewer analytics, comments, and reactions, so recipients can watch asynchronously and respond with timestamped comments or their own reply videos. The platform includes automatic AI-generated transcripts and captions in more than 50 languages, and its post-Atlassian roadmap has emphasized AI features such as auto-generated video titles and summaries, meeting notes and recaps pulled from recordings, and video-to-text automation that turns a recording into a written doc, ticket, or action-item list. Core features include instant screen, camera, and combined recording with no length limit on paid plans; a viewer-facing player with comments, reactions, emoji-timestamped feedback, and view analytics showing who watched how much of a video; automatic transcription and multi-language captions; basic and advanced video editing including trimming, call-to-action overlays, and removing filler words; Loom AI features for auto-generated summaries, meeting notes, and follow-up drafts; workspace and folder organization for teams; and integrations with tools like Slack, Notion, Jira, Confluence, Gmail, and Salesforce. Pricing follows a freemium model: the free Starter plan includes 25 videos with 5-minute screen recordings and unlimited-length meeting recordings, plus transcription in more than 50 languages. The Business plan costs 18 dollars per user per month and unlocks unlimited videos, unlimited recording length, 4K video quality, basic editing, and the ability to remove Loom branding. A Business plus AI plan, Loom's most popular paid tier, costs 24 dollars per user per month and adds AI-powered meeting notes, advanced editing, and video-to-text automation. An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with SSO, SCIM provisioning, Salesforce integration, a 99.95 percent uptime SLA, and dedicated account management; all paid plans include a 14-day free trial, with annual billing saving up to 17 percent. Loom's target audience spans individual professionals doing quick async updates, sales and customer success teams sending personalized video outreach, engineering and product teams documenting bugs or features, and enterprise organizations standardizing async communication to reduce meeting load. Its main competitors include Vidyard, Zoom Clips, Screencastify, and Wistia's Soapbox, along with built-in screen recording features in tools like Slack huddles and Microsoft Teams. Loom differentiates itself through its polished, purpose-built recording and viewer experience, deep AI-assisted editing and summarization, strong integration with Atlassian's collaboration suite post-acquisition, and brand recognition as the category-defining async video tool, though it faces increasing pressure from bundled screen-recording features being added natively into broader collaboration and video-conferencing platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Loom is an async video messaging tool that lets users record and instantly share screen or camera video instead of scheduling a live meeting, founded in San Francisco in 2015 and acquired by Atlassian for 975 million dollars in late 2023.
The platform grew to roughly 25 million users before the acquisition and now operates as part of Atlassian's broader collaboration suite alongside Jira and Confluence.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Loom captures screen and camera simultaneously, auto-uploads the recording, and generates a shareable link with viewer analytics, comments, and reactions.

AI features layered on top of recordings include automatic transcription in more than 50 languages, meeting note generation, and video-to-text automation for turning recordings into written summaries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The free Starter plan allows 25 videos with 5-minute screen recordings, while Business ($18/user/month) and Business plus AI ($24/user/month) unlock unlimited recording length and AI-powered editing features.
An Enterprise plan with SSO, SCIM, and a 99.95 percent uptime SLA is available at custom pricing, and all paid plans include a 14-day free trial.

cons: Free plan caps videos at 25 total and limits screen recordings to 5 minutes

Increasing competition from screen recording features built into Zoom, Slack, and Teams

faqs.question: Who owns Loom now
faqs.answer: Loom is owned by Atlassian, which acquired the company for 975 million dollars in a deal announced October 12, 2023, and completed November 30, 2023.

faqs.question: Is Loom free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Loom's Starter plan is free and includes 25 videos with 5-minute screen recordings and unlimited-length meeting recordings, though paid plans remove those limits.

faqs.question: Who founded Loom
faqs.answer: Loom was founded in 2015 in San Francisco by Vinay Hiremath, Shahed Khan, and Joe Thomas.

faqs.question: How much does Loom Business cost
faqs.answer: Loom Business costs 18 dollars per user per month, while Business plus AI, which adds AI meeting notes and advanced editing, costs 24 dollars per user per month.

faqs.question: What languages does Loom support for transcription
faqs.answer: Loom offers automatic transcription and captions in more than 50 languages.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Loom
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Vidyard, Zoom Clips, Screencastify, and Wistia's Soapbox.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source collaborative decision-making software built by a worker-owned cooperative, unrelated to Loom despite the similar name.
enrichment.seo_title: Loomio Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Loomio review 2026: open-source group decision-making features, pricing plans, its cooperative origins, and how it differs from Loom and Loomly.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Loomio is open-source collaborative decision-making software built and maintained by a worker-owned cooperative based in Wellington, New Zealand. Despite the similar-sounding name, Loomio has no corporate relationship to Loom, the video messaging company acquired by Atlassian, or to Loomly, the social media scheduling platform; the three are entirely separate products built by unrelated companies. Loomio's origins trace to 2011, when activists connected to the Occupy Wellington movement and social entrepreneurs from the Enspiral network in New Zealand identified a shared problem: non-hierarchical groups wanted every member to have a voice in decisions, but traditional top-down voting felt exclusionary while full consensus meetings requiring everyone in a room were too slow to scale. Members of both groups collaborated to design software that could support fast, inclusive, asynchronous decision-making without requiring a physical meeting. The team formally organized as a cooperative and released a Loomio Beta prototype in 2012, then ran a successful international crowdfunding campaign in 2013 that funded development of Loomio 1.0, released in 2014. In 2015 the cooperative raised a round of what it describes as ethical capital to continue expanding the tool's reach. Loomio has operated as a worker-owned cooperative social enterprise ever since, remaining a small team of roughly eight to ten people handling development, support, marketing, and operations out of Wellington. The software itself is released as free software under the GNU Affero General Public License, meaning the source code is publicly available and organizations can self-host it, while the cooperative also offers a hosted, paid version as its primary revenue source. Architecturally, Loomio is a structured group discussion and proposal tool rather than a general chat app: conversations are organized into threads tied to specific groups, and any member can raise a formal proposal with a deadline and a set of response options (agree, abstain, disagree, block), after which Loomio visually summarizes the outcome so the group can see where consensus does or does not exist. This proposal-and-outcome structure is Loomio's core differentiator from generic messaging tools like Slack or forum software like Discourse, which support discussion but have no native mechanism for converting conversation into a recorded, auditable group decision. Core features include threaded group discussions organized by subgroup; formal proposals with customizable response options and deadlines; a visual decision record showing how each member responded and the final outcome; file attachments and rich formatting within threads; email and notification digests so members can participate without checking the app constantly; data export tools so organizations retain full ownership of their discussion and decision history; and, for the self-hosted open-source version, complete control over data location and infrastructure. Pricing follows a freemium-adjacent commercial model layered on top of the open-source core: a Starter plan costs 39 dollars a month (399 dollars billed annually) for one group with up to 30 members, including an onboarding call and email support; a Pro plan costs 99 dollars a month (999 dollars billed annually) for up to 3,000 members with unlimited subgroups; both plans offer significant nonprofit discounts, roughly 25 to 50 percent off, for eligible nonprofits, incorporated societies, volunteer-based, or member-funded organizations, with a choice of USA or Netherlands-based hosting. A Private Loomio tier offers custom pricing for organizations that need dedicated infrastructure, single sign-on, custom branding, and unlimited users and groups. Because pricing is organization-wide rather than strictly per-seat, Loomio tends to be economical for larger nonprofits and cooperatives compared with per-user collaboration tools. Loomio's target audience is distinctly different from typical enterprise SaaS: cooperatives, nonprofits, community organizations, activist and advocacy groups, local government participatory budgeting initiatives, worker-owned businesses, and any group that wants a transparent, non-hierarchical decision-making process. Its competitors are less about direct feature-for-feature rivals and more about adjacent tools that get repurposed for group decisions, including Slack, Discourse, Consensus, and Cobudget, none of which offer Loomio's purpose-built proposal-and-outcome workflow. Loomio differentiates itself through its cooperative ownership structure, open-source licensing, self-hosting option, and its specific focus on making group decisions visible, auditable, and inclusive rather than simply facilitating conversation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Loomio is open-source, purpose-built group decision-making software, built by a worker-owned cooperative in Wellington, New Zealand since 2011, and entirely unrelated to Loom or Loomly despite the similar name.
It grew out of the Occupy Wellington movement and the Enspiral network, whose members needed a tool for fast, inclusive decisions without requiring everyone to meet in person.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Loomio organizes conversation into threaded group discussions and lets any member raise a formal proposal with a deadline and response options like agree, abstain, disagree, or block.
The tool visually summarizes proposal outcomes into an auditable decision record, a structure that distinguishes it from generic chat or forum tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Loomio is released as free, open-source software under the AGPL license and can be self-hosted at no licensing cost.
The cooperative's hosted commercial plans start at $39/month for one group with up to 30 members, scaling to $99/month for up to 3,000 members, with nonprofit discounts available.

features.title: Formal proposals with response options
features.description: Any member can raise a proposal with a deadline and agree, abstain, disagree, or block responses.

features.title: Visual decision record
features.description: Loomio summarizes how each member responded and the final outcome for a transparent, auditable history.

features.title: Open-source and self-hostable

features.title: Nonprofit discounts

faqs.question: Is Loomio related to Loom or Loomly
faqs.answer: No. Loomio, Loom, and Loomly are three completely unrelated products built by different companies; the similar names are coincidental.

faqs.question: Is Loomio free to use
faqs.answer: The core software is open source and can be self-hosted for free under the AGPL license. The cooperative's hosted version starts at $39/month for one group with up to 30 members.

faqs.question: Who runs Loomio
faqs.answer: Loomio is built and operated by Loomio Cooperative, a worker-owned social enterprise based in Wellington, New Zealand, active since 2011.

faqs.question: What makes Loomio different from Slack for decision-making
faqs.answer: Loomio adds a structured proposal feature with deadlines and response options (agree, abstain, disagree, block) and a visual decision record, which general chat tools like Slack do not natively provide.

faqs.question: Does Loomio offer nonprofit pricing
faqs.answer: Yes, eligible nonprofits, incorporated societies, volunteer-based, and member-funded organizations receive discounted rates on both Starter and Pro plans.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Loomio
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source version is available on GitHub and can be self-hosted, giving organizations full control over their data and infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: Social media management platform for planning, approving, and publishing content, now owned by Bending Spoons.
enrichment.company_size: Small to mid-size team, part of Bending Spoons
enrichment.seo_title: Loomly Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Loomly review 2026: social media calendar and approval workflow features, pricing plans, the Bending Spoons acquisition, and alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Loomly is a social media management platform that helps marketing teams, agencies, and brands plan, create, approve, and publish content across multiple social channels from a single visual content calendar. It has no corporate relationship to Loom, the video messaging tool, or Loomio, the decision-making software, despite the similarly styled name; all three are separate companies. Loomly was founded in 2015 by husband-and-wife team Thibaud Clement (CEO) and Noemie Clement (CMO), and the company built its headquarters in New York City. The platform grew steadily through the late 2010s and 2020s by focusing on a specific niche within the crowded social media management category: structured approval workflows and content calendars for marketing teams and agencies managing multiple brands or clients, rather than trying to be the broadest possible all-in-one suite. On January 10, 2025, Loomly was acquired by Bending Spoons, the Italian technology holding company known for acquiring and operationally overhauling established digital products; Bending Spoons' portfolio includes other well-known acquisitions such as Evernote, Meetup, WeTransfer-adjacent tools, Vimeo, StreamYard (formerly Hopin), and Eventbrite, reflecting a broader roll-up strategy of buying mature SaaS brands and improving their unit economics. Financial terms of the Loomly deal were not disclosed. Architecturally, Loomly is built around a shared visual calendar where team members draft posts, attach media, and route them through customizable approval workflows before scheduling and auto-publishing to connected social accounts. Supported channels include the major social networks such as Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), LinkedIn, Pinterest, TikTok, YouTube, and Google Business Profile. On top of scheduling, Loomly layers post ideas and trending-topic suggestions, AI-assisted caption and copy generation, brand guideline reminders that flag deviations before publishing, a unified social inbox for managing comments and messages across channels, and analytics reporting to track post and channel performance. Core features include a shared content calendar with drag-and-drop scheduling; customizable multi-step approval workflows so managers or clients can review and approve posts before they go live; AI-assisted post creation and caption generation; automated post ideas based on trending topics, RSS feeds, and observances; a unified social inbox for replying to comments and messages from one place; performance analytics across connected channels; brand guideline checks that warn users before publishing off-brand content; and library and asset management for reusable media. Pricing is structured in publicly listed tiers: the Base plan costs 42 dollars a month for 10 social accounts, the Standard plan costs 80 dollars a month for 20 accounts, the Advanced plan costs 175 dollars a month for 35 accounts, and the Premium plan costs 369 dollars a month for 50 accounts, with an Enterprise tier available at custom pricing for 61 or more accounts and unlimited users across every tier. Annual billing carries roughly a 25 percent discount compared with monthly billing, and a 7-day free trial is available on paid plans with no credit card required. Loomly's target audience spans small business owners managing their own social presence, in-house marketing teams coordinating multi-brand calendars, and digital marketing agencies that need client approval workflows and multi-account management in one place. Its primary competitors are Buffer, Hootsuite, Sprout Social, Later, and Sprinklr, along with newer AI-native scheduling tools. Loomly differentiates itself through its approval-workflow-centric design, which makes it particularly popular with agencies and teams that need sign-off before anything publishes, its relatively transparent published pricing compared with some enterprise-only competitors, and its post-idea and brand-guideline features that reduce the manual effort of ideation and brand consistency checks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Loomly is a social media management platform built around a shared content calendar and structured approval workflows, founded in New York in 2015 and acquired by Bending Spoons in January 2025.
It is unrelated to Loom or Loomio despite the similar naming pattern, and it competes in the crowded social scheduling category by focusing on approval-driven team and agency workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Loomly's core is a visual calendar where posts move through customizable multi-step approval workflows before being scheduled and auto-published across connected social channels.
AI-assisted caption generation, trending post ideas, a unified social inbox, and brand guideline checks round out the platform beyond basic scheduling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Published pricing scales with the number of connected social accounts: Base at $42/month (10 accounts) up to Premium at $369/month (50 accounts), plus a custom Enterprise tier for 61+ accounts.
All tiers include unlimited users, and annual billing offers roughly a 25 percent discount versus paying monthly.

faqs.question: Is Loomly related to Loom

faqs.question: Who owns Loomly now
faqs.answer: Loomly was acquired by Bending Spoons, an Italian technology holding company, on January 10, 2025. Financial terms were not disclosed.

faqs.question: How much does Loomly cost
faqs.answer: Loomly's Base plan starts at $42/month for 10 social accounts, scaling up to Premium at $369/month for 50 accounts, with a custom Enterprise tier for 61+ accounts.

faqs.question: Does Loomly offer a free trial
faqs.answer: Yes, Loomly offers a 7-day free trial on paid plans with no credit card required, though there is no permanent free tier.

faqs.question: Who founded Loomly
faqs.answer: Loomly was founded in 2015 by Thibaud Clement and Noemie Clement, and is headquartered in New York City.

faqs.answer: Loomly places heavier emphasis on customizable multi-step approval workflows, post idea suggestions, and brand guideline checks, making it especially popular with agencies managing client sign-off.

enrichment.short_description: Email marketing platform purpose-built for SaaS companies, combining marketing, lifecycle, and transactional email.
enrichment.seo_title: Loops Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Loops review 2026: SaaS-focused email marketing features, pricing plans, Y Combinator funding, and how it compares to Customer.io and Mailchimp.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Loops is an email marketing platform built specifically for software-as-a-service companies that need to send marketing campaigns, lifecycle automations, and transactional email from a single product rather than stitching together separate tools for each type of email. The company was founded in 2022 by Chris Frantz and Adam Kaczmarek and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2022 (W22) batch before its public launch. Loops raised 3.2 million dollars in early-stage funding led by Craft Ventures, with participation from Altman Capital, SV Angel, Liquid2, Soma Capital, Box Group, and Twenty-Two Ventures, along with individual angel investors connected to companies like Dropbox, Framer, and Lattice. The company is headquartered in San Francisco and has remained deliberately small, with roughly 11 employees as of mid-2026, reflecting a broader trend among post-ChatGPT-era startups of running lean, product-led teams. Loops was built to address a specific gap that its founders identified in the SaaS tooling market: most email platforms are either optimized purely for marketing broadcasts (like Mailchimp) or purely for developer-triggered transactional and lifecycle email (like Postmark or Customer.io), forcing SaaS companies to run two or three separate tools and reconcile subscriber data between them. Loops instead unifies marketing campaigns, automated lifecycle sequences (such as onboarding drips or trial-expiration nudges), and transactional email (like password resets or receipts) under one contact list and one API, so a SaaS company's entire email program, both broadcast and triggered, lives in a single system. Architecturally, Loops is a no-code-first platform with a visual email editor and automation builder, paired with a developer-friendly API and SDKs for triggering transactional and event-based emails directly from application code. It integrates with common SaaS billing and data tools such as Stripe, Segment, and various no-code and low-code platforms, letting product and growth teams sync user and subscription events into email automations without requiring engineering support for every campaign. Core features include a visual drag-and-drop email editor; audience segmentation based on subscriber properties and behavioral events; automated lifecycle sequences triggered by user actions (signups, trial expiration, upgrades, churn risk); transactional email sending via API with reliable deliverability infrastructure; A/B testing for subject lines and content; Stripe and Segment integrations for syncing billing and product event data; detailed analytics on opens, clicks, and automation performance; and a developer API and SDKs for teams that want to trigger emails directly from their own codebase. Pricing follows a freemium, contact-based model rather than per-seat billing: the Free plan supports up to 1,000 subscribed contacts and 4,000 emails per month with every feature included, aside from a Powered-by-Loops footer branding notice. Paid plans start at 49 dollars a month for 1,000 to 5,000 subscribers and include unlimited sends, full transactional email support, and no feature gating; because there is no per-seat charge, an entire team can use the same paid plan without additional cost, and pricing scales purely with the number of subscribed contacts as a company grows. Loops' target audience is squarely SaaS and software companies, particularly early-stage and growth-stage startups that want marketing, lifecycle, and transactional email consolidated in one tool rather than paying for and maintaining three separate platforms. Its primary competitors include Customer.io, Mailchimp, ConvertKit (rebranded as Kit), Resend, and Postmark, each of which typically specializes in only one segment of the email stack that Loops tries to unify. Loops differentiates itself through this SaaS-specific, all-in-one positioning, its no-per-seat pricing model that removes a common friction point for growing teams, and its founder-led, product-focused brand voice that has resonated strongly within the indie SaaS and Y Combinator startup community, even as it remains a much smaller company than legacy players like Mailchimp.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Loops is an email marketing platform built specifically for SaaS companies, unifying marketing campaigns, lifecycle automations, and transactional email under one contact list and API.
Founded in 2022 by Chris Frantz and Adam Kaczmarek through Y Combinator's Winter 2022 batch, the company has raised $3.2 million led by Craft Ventures and stayed a small, San Francisco-based team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features

Native integrations with Stripe and Segment let product and growth teams sync billing and behavioral events into email automations without engineering support for every campaign.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing

Paid plans start at $49/month for 1,000 to 5,000 subscribers, scale purely with contact count, and include unlimited sends with no per-seat charges.

faqs.answer: Loops is built specifically for SaaS and software companies that want marketing, lifecycle, and transactional email unified in a single platform.

faqs.question: Is Loops free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Loops offers a free plan for up to 1,000 subscribed contacts and 4,000 emails per month with full feature access, aside from a Loops branding footer.

faqs.question: Who founded Loops
faqs.answer: Loops was founded in 2022 by Chris Frantz and Adam Kaczmarek, and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2022 batch.

faqs.question: How much does Loops cost after the free plan
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $49/month for 1,000 to 5,000 subscribers, with pricing scaling by contact count rather than by team seats.

faqs.answer: Yes, Loops provides a developer API and SDKs for triggering transactional and event-based emails directly from application code.

enrichment.short_description: AI-powered app builder that generates full-stack web applications from natural language prompts, formerly known as GPT Engineer.
enrichment.seo_title: Lovable Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lovable review 2026: AI vibe-coding app builder features, credit-based pricing, funding history from GPT Engineer, and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lovable is a Swedish AI-powered app-building platform that lets people describe, in plain language, a web application they want built, then generates and iterates on production-ready, full-stack code in response. The company is headquartered in Stockholm and traces its origins to GPT Engineer, an open-source project created by Anton Osika that used large language models to generate entire codebases from natural-language specifications. Osika, together with Fabian Hedin, later built a commercialized version called the GPT Engineer App, and in December 2024 the founders renamed the product Lovable ahead of a broader public launch, popularizing the term vibe coding for the practice of describing software in natural language and letting AI write the implementation. Lovable's funding trajectory has been unusually fast even by AI-startup standards. The company raised a 7.5 million dollar pre-seed round led by Hummingbird VC and byFounders with participation from major Nordic funds and angel investors, then raised an additional 6.8 million euros in October 2024. In February 2025, Lovable closed a 200 million dollar Series A led by Accel at a valuation of roughly 1.8 billion dollars, an unusually large and fast round reflecting explosive user growth on the back of the vibe-coding trend. The company later raised a 330 million dollar Series B at a 6.6 billion dollar valuation, led by CapitalG and Menlo Ventures' Anthology fund, with participation from Nvidia's venture arm NVentures, Salesforce Ventures, Databricks Ventures, and strategic investors including Atlassian Ventures and HubSpot Ventures, underlining how deeply the AI coding category has attracted both traditional venture capital and corporate strategics. By early 2026 Lovable had grown to roughly 146 full-time employees, was expanding beyond its Stockholm headquarters into offices in London, Boston, and San Francisco, and had been recognized on industry lists such as CNBC's 2026 Disruptor 50 ranking. Architecturally, Lovable operates as a conversational, agentic code-generation environment: users describe features, pages, or entire applications in natural language, and Lovable's underlying AI models generate and modify a real, editable full-stack codebase, typically using React on the frontend and Supabase for backend services like authentication, databases, and storage, rather than producing a locked black-box output. Generated apps can be connected to custom domains, deployed directly from the platform, and extended with further prompts as requirements evolve, positioning Lovable as much closer to an AI-native IDE and hosting platform than a simple prototyping tool. Core features include natural-language-to-full-stack-code generation; live visual editing alongside the underlying code; built-in Supabase integration for authentication, databases, and storage; one-click custom domain connection and deployment; AI-assisted debugging and iterative refinement through follow-up prompts; team collaboration with unlimited workspace members on every plan; a credit-based usage system that meters both app-building actions and the compute used by shipped apps; and enterprise features like single sign-on and a security center on higher tiers. Pricing runs on a credit model rather than flat per-seat pricing: the Free plan grants 5 build credits daily (up to 30 a month) plus 20 monthly Cloud credits for hosting, sufficient for light experimentation. The Pro plan costs 25 dollars a month (21 dollars a month billed annually) and includes 100 monthly credits plus daily bonus credits, full Supabase access, and custom domains. The Business plan costs 50 dollars a month (42 dollars a month billed annually) and adds single sign-on and a security center for team deployments, while Enterprise pricing is custom. Because Lovable meters usage across both the app-building process and the ongoing cloud and AI costs of apps once they are shipped and used by end customers, actual costs can exceed the base subscription price for actively used applications, a dynamic that has drawn some user criticism around cost predictability. Lovable's target audience spans non-technical founders and solo builders who want to ship a working product without hiring engineers, product managers and designers who want to prototype quickly, and, increasingly, professional development teams using it to accelerate scaffolding and iteration. Its main competitors are other AI coding and app-generation tools, including Vercel's v0, StackBlitz's Bolt.new, Replit, Cursor, and Base44. Lovable differentiates itself through its focus on generating complete, deployable full-stack applications rather than isolated code snippets or UI components, its tight native integration with Supabase for real backend functionality, and the speed and scale of its funding and user growth, which has made it one of the most prominent brands in the broader vibe-coding and AI app-generation category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lovable is a Stockholm-based AI app builder that generates full-stack, deployable web applications from natural-language prompts, evolving out of Anton Osika's open-source GPT Engineer project before being renamed Lovable in December 2024.

The company has raised funding at an extraordinary pace, reaching a $6.6 billion valuation on a $330 million Series B backed by CapitalG, Menlo Ventures' Anthology fund, Nvidia's NVentures, Salesforce Ventures, and Databricks Ventures.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users describe an app or feature in plain language, and Lovable generates and iteratively edits a real, editable codebase, typically React on the frontend paired with Supabase for backend services.
Generated apps can be deployed with custom domains directly from the platform, with further changes made through follow-up prompts or direct code edits.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lovable uses a credit-based pricing model: Free includes daily build credits and monthly Cloud credits, Pro costs $25/month for 100 monthly credits, and Business costs $50/month with SSO and a security center.
A second, usage-based layer of Cloud and AI costs applies to shipped apps on top of the subscription, which can make total costs higher than the base plan price suggests.

enrichment.short_description: AI voice generation platform offering text-to-speech, voice cloning, and video editing through its Genny product.
enrichment.seo_title: LOVO Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LOVO AI review 2026: text-to-speech and voice cloning features in Genny, pricing plans, funding history, and how it compares to ElevenLabs and Murf.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LOVO is an AI voice generation platform that produces realistic text-to-speech voiceovers and cloned voices for video, e-learning, advertising, gaming, and audiobook production. The company was founded in Berkeley, California in November 2019 by co-founders Tom Lee and Charlie Choi, initially operating under the name Orbis AI before rebranding to LOVO as its speech-synthesis technology matured. LOVO has raised a total of roughly 11.5 million dollars across two funding rounds: a 4.5 million dollar Pre-Series A round led by Kakao Entertainment, with participation from Kakao Investment, LG CNS, previous backer SkyDeck Fund (UC Berkeley's startup accelerator), and individual investors including a former DoorDash finance executive, followed by a 2 million dollar extension to that round led by crypto-focused venture firm Hashed. Other investors in the company include Animoca Brands, AppWorks, Cherubic Ventures, Dragonfly Capital Partners, and Ethereal Ventures. The company has remained a lean team, reported at somewhere between roughly 14 and 22 employees depending on the source and date, while claiming a user base of more than two million creators and professionals worldwide. LOVO's flagship product is Genny, an integrated platform that combines an AI voice generator with a text-to-speech engine and a built-in online video editor, so users can generate a voiceover and pair it with visuals in the same workspace rather than exporting audio to a separate video tool. Architecturally, Genny is built around large-scale neural text-to-speech and voice-cloning models trained to produce natural-sounding prosody, emotion, and pacing rather than the flatter, more robotic output associated with earlier-generation TTS systems; the platform offers more than 500 distinct AI voices across roughly 100 languages and dialects, along with a voice-cloning feature that lets users create a custom synthetic voice from a short sample recording, subject to consent and usage safeguards. Core features include text-to-speech generation across 500-plus voices and around 100 languages; instant voice cloning from short audio samples; an integrated video editor for pairing generated voiceovers with visuals, captions, and stock media; emotion and style controls that let users adjust tone, pacing, and delivery for a given voice; an API for developers who want to embed LOVO's speech synthesis into their own applications and products; commercial usage rights on paid plans for use in ads, games, and monetized content; and collaborative workspace features for teams producing voice content at scale. Pricing in 2026 runs on three consumer-facing tiers plus a custom enterprise option, alongside a 14-day free trial that includes roughly 20 minutes of generated audio. The Basic plan costs 24 dollars a month, the Pro plan costs 48 dollars a month, and the Pro+ plan costs 149 dollars a month, with each successive tier increasing monthly minutes of generated audio, the number of available voices and languages, cloning allowances, and commercial usage rights; an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing for high-volume or API-heavy use cases with dedicated support. LOVO's target audience includes independent content creators and YouTubers producing voiceovers without hiring voice actors, corporate learning and development teams building e-learning and training narration, marketing and advertising teams producing multilingual ad voiceovers at scale, game studios needing character voice lines, and audiobook and podcast producers. Its primary competitors in the AI voice generation category include ElevenLabs, Murf AI, Speechify, WellSaid Labs, and Play.ht, each competing on some combination of voice realism, language coverage, cloning fidelity, and pricing. LOVO differentiates itself through the breadth of its language and voice library, its bundled video editor that removes the need for a separate editing tool, and its relatively accessible entry-level pricing compared with some enterprise-focused competitors, while facing ongoing competitive pressure from ElevenLabs and other rivals that have set a fast pace of improvement in voice realism and emotional expressiveness across the broader generative-audio category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LOVO is an AI voice generation company founded in Berkeley, California in 2019 by Tom Lee and Charlie Choi, originally operating as Orbis AI before rebranding.
Its flagship product, Genny, combines text-to-speech, voice cloning, and an integrated video editor, serving more than two million creators and professionals worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Genny offers more than 500 AI voices across roughly 100 languages, built on neural text-to-speech models designed for natural prosody and emotional delivery.
Instant voice cloning from short audio samples, an integrated video editor, and an API for developers round out the platform beyond basic narration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: LOVO's 2026 pricing includes a 14-day free trial with about 20 minutes of audio, then Basic at $24/month, Pro at $48/month, and Pro+ at $149/month.
Each tier increases monthly audio minutes, voice and language access, cloning allowances, and commercial usage rights, with custom Enterprise pricing for high-volume needs.

pros: Large library of 500+ voices across roughly 100 languages and dialects
Bundled video editor removes the need for a separate editing tool
Voice cloning feature enables custom synthetic voices from short samples
Entry-level pricing is more accessible than some enterprise-focused competitors
Developer API supports embedding voice generation into other products
cons: Faces intense competitive pressure from fast-moving rivals like ElevenLabs on voice realism
Small company size relative to the scale of its two million-plus user base
Higher tiers (Pro+ at $149/month) are a significant jump from entry-level pricing
Voice cloning features carry consent and misuse risks common to the category
Free trial is limited to about 20 minutes of generated audio

faqs.question: What is LOVO's main product called
faqs.answer: LOVO's flagship product is Genny, which combines AI voice generation, text-to-speech, and an integrated video editor.

faqs.question: Who founded LOVO
faqs.answer: LOVO was founded in November 2019 in Berkeley, California, by Tom Lee and Charlie Choi, originally under the name Orbis AI.

faqs.question: How much does LOVO cost
faqs.answer: LOVO's 2026 pricing includes Basic at $24/month, Pro at $48/month, and Pro+ at $149/month, plus a custom Enterprise tier and a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: Does LOVO support voice cloning
faqs.answer: Yes, LOVO offers instant voice cloning that creates a custom synthetic voice from a short audio sample.

faqs.question: How many languages does LOVO support
faqs.answer: LOVO offers more than 500 AI voices across roughly 100 languages and dialects.

faqs.question: Who are LOVO's main competitors
faqs.answer: LOVO's main competitors in AI voice generation include ElevenLabs, Murf AI, Speechify, WellSaid Labs, and Play.ht.
